MUHS protocols mandate thorough third-stage assessment as a core midwifery competency, with prompt surgical evacuation arranged when RPOC are confirmed to prevent life-threatening haemorrhage and sepsis.

106.intro:
Introduction

Perineal care in the puerperium encompasses the hygienic, therapeutic, and rehabilitative measures applied to the perineum following childbirth, particularly when episiotomy, perineal tears, or instrumented delivery has occurred. The perineum is highly susceptible to infection and delayed healing due to its proximity to the anorectal area, moisture, and lochia, making diligent care a cornerstone of postnatal nursing practice. Effective perineal care reduces the risk of wound infection, dehiscence, and long-term morbidity such as dyspareunia and pelvic floor dysfunction.
106.def:
Definition

Perineal Care (Puerperium): A set of nursing interventions aimed at maintaining cleanliness, promoting healing, preventing infection, and providing comfort to the perineal region following vaginal delivery, including wound assessment, hygiene practices, pain relief measures, and patient education throughout the postnatal period.
106.conclusion:
Conclusion

The nurse midwife must assess the perineum at every postnatal visit using the REEDA scale (Redness, Oedema, Ecchymosis, Discharge, Approximation) and educate the mother on front-to-back cleansing, pad hygiene, and early reporting of signs of infection. Adequate pain management through analgesics, ice packs, and sitz baths promotes compliance with hygiene and mobility. MUHS & INC recognise perineal care as a fundamental intrapartum and postnatal nursing skill that directly influences maternal recovery and quality of life.

107.intro:
Introduction

Subinvolution of the uterus is a significant postnatal complication in which the normal process of uterine regression is delayed or incomplete, predisposing the mother to secondary postpartum haemorrhage and puerperal infection. It is commonly encountered in resource-limited settings in India where antenatal anaemia, malnutrition, and multiparity increase susceptibility. The nurse midwife is ideally positioned to detect subinvolution during routine postnatal assessments and initiate timely referral.
107.conclusion:
Conclusion

Vigilant postnatal monitoring of uterine fundal height, lochia, and uterine consistency by the nurse midwife remains the most effective strategy for early detection of subinvolution. Prompt medical management with uterotonics and treatment of underlying infection can prevent life-threatening haemorrhage. MUHS postnatal care protocols mandate that every mother be assessed for signs of subinvolution before discharge and at each follow-up visit.

110.def:
Definition

Antenatal Care (ANC): Systematic healthcare provided to a pregnant woman from confirmation of pregnancy until the onset of labour, comprising regular clinical assessments, investigations, health education, and preventive interventions to ensure safe motherhood and optimal fetal outcome.

High-Risk Pregnancy: A pregnancy in which the mother, fetus, or neonate has a significantly greater-than-average likelihood of morbidity or mortality, requiring intensified ANC contacts beyond the standard schedule.

124.intro:
Introduction

Antepartum haemorrhage (APH) after 28 weeks of gestation is one of the most dangerous emergencies in obstetric nursing. Placenta praevia and abruptio placenta are its two major placental causes, together accounting for the majority of APH-related maternal and perinatal deaths in India. Every nurse midwife must be able to define, classify, and differentiate these conditions to guide immediate life-saving action.

125.intro:
Introduction

Placenta praevia is a significant cause of antepartum haemorrhage and is closely associated with maternal anaemia, preterm delivery, and perinatal mortality. In India, the rising rate of caesarean sections has increased the incidence of abnormal placentation, making this a clinically important and frequently examined MUHS topic. The nurse midwife’s primary responsibility is to recognise the characteristic painless bleeding, prevent PV examination, and facilitate timely obstetric and surgical management.

126.intro:
Introduction

Abruptio placenta (accidental haemorrhage) is a leading cause of maternal and perinatal death in India, particularly in women with pregnancy-induced hypertension. Unlike placenta praevia, it presents with severe abdominal pain and a woody-hard uterus, and concealed haemorrhage may cause shock that is disproportionate to any visible bleeding. Prompt recognition by the nurse midwife, rapid resuscitation, and urgent delivery are essential to prevent maternal death from haemorrhage and DIC.

127.intro:
Introduction

Antepartum haemorrhage (APH) complicates 3–5% of all pregnancies and is a major contributor to maternal and perinatal mortality in India. While many causes exist — from benign cervical erosion to catastrophic placental separation — it is the two placental causes (placenta praevia and abruptio placenta) that demand immediate emergency nursing action. A clear understanding of APH definition, its causes, and its clinical differentiation is a cornerstone of safe obstetric nursing practice.

128.intro:
Introduction

Antepartum haemorrhage is defined as bleeding from the genital tract after 28 completed weeks of pregnancy and before delivery of the fetus. It is encountered in 3–5% of pregnancies in India and requires systematic enumeration of its causes to determine management priorities. This is a frequently tested short-answer topic in MUHS obstetric nursing examinations, requiring a clear, classified list presented in a structured format.

129.intro:
Introduction

Abruptio placenta, or accidental haemorrhage, is a dangerous obstetric emergency characterised by premature separation of the normally-sited placenta. In India, it is closely associated with pregnancy-induced hypertension and poses serious risks including concealed haemorrhage, DIC, and fetal death. The nurse midwife must recognise its clinical signs early, initiate resuscitation without delay, and prepare for urgent delivery — making this a high-priority topic in both practice and MUHS examinations.

130.intro:
Introduction

Differentiating placenta praevia from abruptio placenta is the most frequently repeated question in MUHS obstetric nursing examinations on antepartum haemorrhage. Although both conditions cause bleeding after 28 weeks of gestation, their clinical presentations, underlying pathology, uterine findings, and management strategies are fundamentally different. A well-constructed comparison table earns full marks and is highly favoured by examiners.
130.def:
Definition

Placenta Praevia: Implantation of the placenta wholly or partially in the lower uterine segment (LUS), partially or completely covering the internal os, causing painless antepartum haemorrhage (APH).

Abruptio Placenta (Accidental Haemorrhage): Premature separation of a normally-sited placenta from its uterine attachment after 28 weeks and before delivery of the fetus, resulting in painful APH with a retroplacental haematoma.

132.intro:
Introduction

Iron deficiency anaemia (IDA) is the most prevalent nutritional deficiency in pregnancy in India, affecting over 50% of pregnant women according to NFHS-5 (2019–21) data. The unique demands of pregnancy — expanding maternal red cell mass and fetal iron requirements — create a negative iron balance that dietary intake alone cannot meet in resource-limited settings. Understanding the causes, fetal effects, and stepwise management of IDA is essential for every MUHS nursing student and for reducing India’s burden of preventable maternal and perinatal mortality.

133.intro:
Introduction

Anaemia in pregnancy is defined by WHO as haemoglobin below 11 g/dL and is the most prevalent medical complication of pregnancy in India, affecting nearly 52% of pregnant women (NFHS-5). It is a leading indirect cause of maternal death and is strongly associated with preterm birth, low birth weight, and postpartum haemorrhage. A systematic understanding of its classification, causes, and management — including the emergency nursing care of severe anaemia — is a mandatory examination and clinical competency for MUHS nursing students.

137.intro:
Introduction

Pre-eclampsia is a multisystem pregnancy-specific disorder affecting 5–10% of pregnancies worldwide and contributing significantly to maternal deaths in India. It is characterised by hypertension and end-organ involvement developing after 20 weeks of gestation, and can rapidly escalate to the convulsive, life-threatening state of eclampsia. Early nursing recognition of diagnostic criteria and danger signs, along with vigilant monitoring and timely medical intervention, are the cornerstones of preventing maternal and fetal morbidity.

138.intro:
Introduction

Pregnancy-induced hypertension (PIH) is a spectrum of hypertensive disorders unique to pregnancy that accounts for approximately 16% of maternal deaths in India. From the initial failure of trophoblast invasion through generalised endothelial dysfunction to multi-organ vasospasm, the pathophysiology of PIH explains every clinical feature and every nursing intervention. A thorough grasp of this cascade is essential for MUHS examinations and for the bedside nurse managing women with hypertension in pregnancy.

139.intro:
Introduction

Pregnancy-induced hypertension (PIH) remains one of the foremost causes of maternal and perinatal mortality in India, with eclampsia representing its most lethal complication. Magnesium sulphate (MgSO4) has transformed the management of severe PIH and eclampsia, and its correct administration and monitoring is a core clinical skill for every nurse midwife in Indian obstetric units. This question integrates PIH classification, its serious complications, MgSO4 pharmacology, and the nurse’s critical antenatal and intranatal care responsibilities.

140.intro:
Introduction

PIH and its most dangerous manifestation — eclampsia — constitute one of the most important emergency scenarios in MUHS obstetric nursing examinations. Eclampsia, characterised by convulsions superimposed on pre-eclampsia, is responsible for a significant proportion of preventable maternal deaths in India. Skilled nursing assessment of the four stages of convulsion, immediate seizure management, and timely administration of MgSO4 are critical competencies for every nurse midwife working in Indian tertiary care facilities.
140.def:
Definition

PIH (Pregnancy-Induced Hypertension): Development of BP ≥140/90 mmHg (on two occasions at least 4 hours apart) after 20 weeks of gestation in a previously normotensive woman, with or without proteinuria.

Eclampsia: The occurrence of one or more generalised tonic-clonic convulsions in a woman with pre-eclampsia, not attributable to any other cause such as epilepsy or meningitis.

141.intro:
Introduction

Pre-eclampsia and eclampsia are the most severe hypertensive complications of pregnancy and are among the leading causes of maternal mortality in India. Pre-eclampsia (hypertension with proteinuria after 20 weeks) can deteriorate rapidly into eclampsia (tonic-clonic convulsions), which constitutes a life-threatening obstetric emergency requiring immediate airway protection, seizure control with MgSO4, and urgent delivery. Skilled nursing recognition of the four stages of convulsion and prompt initiation of the MgSO4 protocol are critical competencies for every MUHS nursing graduate.

142.intro:
Introduction

The roll-over test is a simple, non-invasive antenatal screening tool used to identify women at increased risk of developing pre-eclampsia. It is performed between 20 and 28 weeks of gestation and exploits the physiological sensitivity of the renin-angiotensin system in women predisposed to PIH. Early identification of a positive test enables the nurse midwife to intensify antenatal surveillance and counsel the woman on danger signs, potentially preventing progression to eclampsia.
142.def:
Definition

Roll-Over Test (Gant Test): An antenatal screening test for pre-eclampsia in which the diastolic blood pressure is measured in the left lateral position and then repeated after the woman rolls to the supine position. A rise in diastolic BP of ≥20 mmHg on turning supine constitutes a positive test, indicating increased vascular sensitivity to angiotensin II and a heightened risk of developing gestational hypertension or pre-eclampsia.
142.conclusion:
Conclusion

The roll-over test is a valuable bedside screening tool that the nurse midwife can perform at every antenatal visit between 20 and 28 weeks. A positive result (diastolic rise ≥20 mmHg on turning supine) must prompt the nurse to intensify BP monitoring, urine protein surveillance, and maternal education on danger signs, in accordance with MUHS and INC standards of high-risk pregnancy care. Early detection through simple tests like the roll-over test remains central to reducing India’s burden of eclampsia-related maternal mortality.

146.def:
Definition

Heart Disease in Pregnancy: The presence of a structural or functional cardiac disorder — congenital or acquired — in a pregnant woman, placing her at increased risk of maternal cardiac decompensation due to the physiological haemodynamic demands of pregnancy (30–50% increase in cardiac output, expanded blood volume, and increased heart rate).

NYHA Classification: Class I — no symptoms; Class II — symptoms on moderate exertion; Class III — symptoms on minimal exertion; Class IV — symptoms at rest. Classes III–IV carry high maternal mortality.

147.def:
Definition

Heart Disease in Pregnancy: Any pre-existing (congenital or rheumatic) or pregnancy-acquired cardiac disorder that impairs the woman’s ability to tolerate the physiological cardiovascular demands of pregnancy, labour, and the puerperium. Classified functionally by NYHA Class I–IV; Class III–IV and pulmonary hypertension are associated with the highest maternal mortality (>50%).

148.def:
Definition

Ovarian Tumour in Pregnancy: A mass arising from ovarian tissue discovered coincidentally or symptomatically during pregnancy. Found in approximately 1 in 100–500 pregnancies, most are benign functional cysts (corpus luteum, follicular, dermoid) that resolve by 16 weeks; persistent complex or solid masses require surgical evaluation to exclude malignancy.

151.intro:
Introduction

TORCH infections are a group of vertically transmitted pathogens that cross the placenta and cause a spectrum of congenital disease ranging from subclinical infection to severe organ damage, growth restriction, and neonatal death. They represent an important preventable cause of congenital anomalies in India, where rubella immunity may be incomplete in unvaccinated women and sanitation-related exposures to toxoplasmosis remain common. Nurses play a critical role in pre-conception counselling, antenatal screening, and educating pregnant women on hygiene-based prevention strategies.

152.def:
Definition

Monilial Vaginitis (Vulvovaginal Candidiasis): A fungal infection of the vagina and vulva caused by Candida albicans (in over 90% of cases), characterised by intense pruritus vulvae, a thick curdy white (cottage cheese-like) non-foul-smelling vaginal discharge, and vulval erythema. It is 2–3 times more prevalent in pregnancy due to elevated oestrogen and increased vaginal glycogen, which promote fungal overgrowth.

155.intro:
Introduction

Rh incompatibility is a preventable cause of haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN) that continues to cause perinatal morbidity in India. When an Rh-negative mother carries an Rh-positive fetus, feto-maternal haemorrhage triggers production of anti-D IgG antibodies that cross the placenta in subsequent pregnancies and destroy fetal red blood cells. The nurse midwife occupies a pivotal role in prevention through timely Rh typing, antibody screening at booking, and administration of Anti-D immunoglobulin at key antenatal and postnatal time points.

156.intro:
Introduction

Rh incompatibility arises when an Rh-negative mother develops anti-D antibodies after exposure to Rh-positive fetal blood, placing future Rh-positive pregnancies at risk of haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN). Management depends entirely on whether sensitisation has already occurred: unsensitised women can be effectively protected with prophylactic Anti-D immunoglobulin, while already-sensitised women require intensive fetal surveillance including serial antibody titres and MCA Doppler studies. Understanding this two-pathway management approach is a core MUHS examination and clinical competency.
156.def:
Definition

Rh Incompatibility: A condition arising when an Rh-negative mother is pregnant with an Rh-positive fetus (whose Rh-positive red blood cells, having entered the maternal circulation, stimulate production of anti-D IgG antibodies). These antibodies persist across pregnancies and in subsequent Rh-positive pregnancies cross the placenta to cause haemolytic disease of the fetus and newborn (HDFN), ranging from mild jaundice to hydrops fetalis.

157.intro:
Introduction

Rh-isoimmunisation is the process by which an Rh-negative mother mounts an immune response against Rh(D)-positive fetal red blood cells that have entered her circulation. This sensitisation is largely preventable with Anti-D immunoglobulin, yet it remains a cause of perinatal morbidity in India due to gaps in antenatal care uptake and inadequate prophylaxis. Understanding the mechanism, clinical consequences, and prevention of Rh-isoimmunisation is a fundamental nursing competency endorsed by both MUHS and the Indian Nursing Council (INC).

158.intro:
Introduction

Injection Anti-D (anti-D immunoglobulin) is the most effective preventive intervention against Rh isoimmunisation, transforming what was once a leading cause of perinatal death into a largely preventable condition. Every Rh-negative pregnant woman seen at an Indian antenatal clinic is a potential candidate for Anti-D prophylaxis, and the nurse midwife is responsible for identifying these women, checking their sensitisation status, and administering the injection correctly and within the critical time window. This is a high-yield short-answer topic in MUHS obstetric nursing examinations.

161.intro:
Introduction

Polyhydramnios, defined as an excess of amniotic fluid, complicates approximately 1–2% of all pregnancies in India and is closely associated with gestational diabetes mellitus, foetal gastrointestinal or neurological anomalies, and multiple gestation. The overdistended uterus predisposes the mother to preterm labour, malpresentation, cord prolapse, and postpartum haemorrhage, making early recognition and prompt management essential in obstetric nursing practice. The nurse midwife plays a pivotal role in identifying suspicious uterine enlargement at ANC visits and initiating timely referral to a higher centre.

163.intro:
Introduction

Oligohydramnios — a deficiency of amniotic fluid — is a significant obstetric finding that directly endangers foetal well-being by removing the protective cushion around the umbilical cord and restricting pulmonary development. It occurs in approximately 4–8% of pregnancies in India and is frequently associated with foetal renal anomalies, placental insufficiency, premature rupture of membranes, and post-term pregnancy. The nurse midwife must promptly detect oligohydramnios during routine ANC ultrasound surveillance and implement evidence-based nursing interventions to minimise foetal morbidity and mortality.

164.intro:
Introduction

Oligohydramnios in an intrapartum patient represents one of the most demanding clinical situations for the nurse midwife, as cord compression and foetal hypoxia can develop rapidly once labour is established. Reduced amniotic fluid volume removes the buffering effect around the umbilical cord during contractions, leading to variable decelerations and progressive foetal acidosis if undetected. Active first-stage management — encompassing continuous CTG monitoring, maternal hydration, amnioinfusion, and readiness for emergency caesarean section — is critical to achieving a safe outcome for both mother and baby.
164.def:
Definition

Oligohydramnios: A condition in which the amniotic fluid volume is abnormally reduced for gestational age. Diagnosed on ultrasound when the Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI) is <5 cm or the Single Deepest Pocket (SDP) is <2 cm. Normal AFI at term ranges from 8–18 cm.

165.intro:
Introduction

Oligohydramnios is a clinically important condition in Indian obstetric practice, with an incidence of 4–8% of all pregnancies, and carries a significantly elevated risk of perinatal morbidity and mortality when unrecognised. The deficiency of amniotic fluid arises from diverse foetal, placental, and maternal causes and exerts profound adverse effects on the developing foetus, particularly on pulmonary maturation and umbilical cord integrity. Early diagnosis through serial AFI measurement at ANC visits, followed by systematic nursing management, is fundamental to reducing preventable perinatal deaths in resource-limited Indian healthcare settings.
165.def:
Definition

Oligohydramnios: An abnormal reduction in amniotic fluid volume below the expected range for gestational age, diagnosed when the Amniotic Fluid Index (AFI) is <5 cm or the Single Deepest Pocket (SDP) is <2 cm on ultrasound. Incidence: 4–8%; associated with increased perinatal morbidity and mortality.

166.intro:
Introduction

Oligohydramnios is a potentially life-threatening obstetric complication characterised by an abnormally low volume of amniotic fluid that compromises foetal development, cord perfusion, and pulmonary growth. In the Indian healthcare context, where access to serial ultrasound may be limited, the nurse midwife must rely on clinical indicators such as a uterus that measures small for gestational age and a conspicuously easy palpation of foetal parts to raise suspicion and arrange timely imaging. Prompt identification and appropriate nursing intervention are essential to prevent cord compression emergencies and adverse neonatal outcomes.

167.intro:
Introduction

Multiple pregnancy, occurring in approximately 1 in 80 spontaneous conceptions in India — with rates rising due to assisted reproductive technologies — carries substantially higher maternal and foetal risks than singleton pregnancy. The overdistended uterus, doubled nutritional demands, increased placental mass, and potential for abnormal foetal presentation collectively elevate the rates of preterm labour, pregnancy-induced hypertension, anaemia, postpartum haemorrhage, and neonatal intensive care admissions. The nurse midwife must be proficient in early recognition of multiple gestation, enumeration of its causes, and skilled management of twin delivery to safeguard both mother and neonates.

168.intro:
Introduction

Multiple pregnancy involves two or more developing foetuses in the uterus simultaneously, with twins being the most common form; the incidence in India is approximately 1 in 80 spontaneous pregnancies and is increasing with the use of ovulation induction agents and in-vitro fertilisation. The type of twin pregnancy — determined by zygosity and chorionicity — is the single most important prognostic factor, as monochorionic twins share a placenta and are at risk of twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS), cord entanglement, and discordant growth. The nurse midwife must understand the classification of twins and the differentiated antenatal and intranatal management required for each type to ensure safe outcomes.
168.def:
Definition

Multiple Pregnancy: A pregnancy in which two or more foetuses develop simultaneously within the uterus. Twins are classified as dizygotic (fraternal; 70%; from two separate ova) or monozygotic (identical; 30%; from one fertilised ovum that divides). Chorionicity — determined by the timing of the split — defines the risk category: DCDA (safest), MCDA (TTTS risk), MCMA (highest risk).

169.intro:
Introduction

Multiple pregnancy is associated with a disproportionately high burden of obstetric complications — affecting both the mother and the foetuses — making it one of the most high-risk categories in Indian obstetric nursing practice. The combination of uterine overdistension, shared placental circulation in monochorionic pregnancies, increased nutritional demands, and greater foetal number collectively predisposes to a spectrum of serious maternal and foetal complications that require proactive identification and management. The nurse midwife must be vigilant about these complications from early pregnancy onwards to facilitate timely referral and reduce maternal and perinatal mortality.
169.def:
Definition

Complications of Multiple Pregnancy: The additional maternal and foetal risks that arise specifically because of the presence of two or more foetuses in the uterus. Maternal complications include anaemia, pregnancy-induced hypertension, polyhydramnios, preterm labour, and postpartum haemorrhage; foetal complications include prematurity, IUGR, twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome (TTTS), malpresentation, and cord entanglement.
169.conclusion:
Conclusion

Multiple pregnancy represents a major high-risk obstetric category in which the nurse midwife’s role in early detection, vigilant ANC monitoring, and timely referral is paramount. Key MUHS examination keywords include twin-to-twin transfusion syndrome, preterm labour, postpartum haemorrhage, and active management of the third stage. Early recognition of complications and coordinated care at a tertiary centre with NICU facilities are essential to improving perinatal outcomes in multiple pregnancies.

170.intro:
Introduction

The umbilical cord is the lifeline connecting the developing foetus to the placenta, carrying oxygenated blood and nutrients via one umbilical vein and returning deoxygenated blood via two umbilical arteries, all protected by the gelatinous Wharton’s jelly. Abnormalities of the umbilical cord — ranging from cord prolapse and velamentous insertion to single umbilical artery and vasa praevia — are clinically significant because they can cause acute foetal compromise, haemorrhage, or neonatal congenital anomalies. The nurse midwife must be competent in detecting CTG patterns suggestive of cord compression, responding to cord emergencies, and examining the delivered cord for structural abnormalities.
170.def:
Definition

Umbilical Cord: A flexible, rope-like structure approximately 50–55 cm in length and 1–2 cm in diameter, connecting the foetus to the placenta. It normally contains two umbilical arteries (carrying deoxygenated blood from foetus to placenta) and one umbilical vein (carrying oxygenated blood from placenta to foetus), embedded in Wharton’s jelly and covered by a single layer of amnion.

171.intro:
Introduction

Intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR) — also termed foetal growth restriction (FGR) — is one of the most significant causes of perinatal morbidity and mortality in India, where high prevalence of maternal anaemia, malnutrition, and pregnancy-induced hypertension create a particularly unfavourable uterine environment. A foetus affected by IUGR fails to achieve its genetically determined growth potential, resulting in an estimated foetal weight below the 10th percentile for gestational age, and is at heightened risk of intrauterine hypoxia, stillbirth, and long-term neurodevelopmental impairment. The nurse midwife must be skilled in surveillance fundal height measurements, interpretation of Doppler and biophysical profile findings, and timely escalation of care to prevent preventable perinatal deaths.

172.intro:
Introduction

Intrauterine growth retardation (IUGR) is a leading contributor to perinatal morbidity and mortality globally, and is particularly prevalent in India due to the high burden of maternal undernutrition, anaemia, and hypertensive disorders of pregnancy. The condition is not a single entity but encompasses two distinct types — symmetric and asymmetric — that differ in their aetiology, timing of onset, ultrasound findings, and prognosis. A clear understanding of these types is essential for MUHS nursing examinations and for the nurse midwife to tailor antenatal surveillance and delivery planning appropriately.
172.def:
Definition

Intrauterine Growth Retardation (IUGR) / Foetal Growth Restriction (FGR): A condition in which the foetus fails to achieve its genetically predetermined growth potential, resulting in an estimated foetal weight (EFW) or abdominal circumference (AC) below the 10th percentile for gestational age on ultrasound. It is classified into Symmetric (Type I, 20%) — affecting all foetal organs equally from early pregnancy — and Asymmetric (Type II, 80%) — sparing the brain with preferential reduction in abdominal circumference, caused by third-trimester placental insufficiency.

173.intro:
Introduction

Intrauterine foetal death (IUFD) — defined as the death of a foetus at or after 28 weeks of gestation before delivery — is a devastating obstetric complication that demands both clinical acumen and profound compassionate care from the nurse midwife. In India, the stillbirth rate remains one of the highest globally, driven predominantly by pregnancy-induced hypertension, placental abruption, severe anaemia, and uncontrolled diabetes. The nurse midwife must be competent in recognising the clinical and ultrasound indicators of IUFD, assisting with induction of labour, monitoring for life-threatening coagulopathy, and providing sensitive bereavement support to the grieving family.
173.def:
Definition

Intrauterine Foetal Death (IUFD) / Stillbirth: The death of a foetus at or after 28 weeks of gestation (or birth weight ≥1000 g) occurring before complete expulsion or extraction from the mother, irrespective of the duration of pregnancy. Diagnosed by absent foetal cardiac activity on ultrasound. Confirmed on clinical examination by absence of foetal movements, absent foetal heart sounds on Doppler, and radiological signs (Spalding’s sign: overlapping skull bones).

174.intro:
Introduction

Obstructed labour is one of the most dangerous complications of the intrapartum period, accounting for a significant proportion of maternal deaths and stillbirths in India, particularly in rural and resource-limited settings. It occurs when a mechanical barrier prevents descent of the presenting part despite adequate uterine contractions. The nurse midwife must recognise its early warning signs—including Bandl’s ring and moulding +3—and initiate emergency referral without delay, as every minute lost increases the risk of uterine rupture, vesicovaginal fistula, and maternal death.

175.intro:
Introduction

Prolonged labour is a common yet preventable cause of maternal and neonatal morbidity in India, occurring when the active phase of labour extends beyond the expected duration due to abnormalities of Power, Passage, or Passenger. The partograph, mandated under the Janani Suraksha Yojana (JSY) protocol, is the nurse midwife’s primary tool for early detection. Timely identification and appropriate intervention—whether oxytocin augmentation or caesarean section—can prevent progression to the far more dangerous obstructed labour.
175.def:
Definition

Prolonged Labour: Labour in which the active phase (cervical dilation ≥4 cm) lasts more than 12 hours in a primigravida or more than 8 hours in a multigravida, with cervical dilation progressing at less than 1 cm per hour as assessed on the partograph.

176.intro:
Introduction

Understanding the causes of prolonged labour is fundamental to intrapartum nursing care, as early identification of the underlying cause determines the correct management pathway. In India, where grand multiparity and malnutrition remain prevalent, causes related to the Passage (contracted pelvis) and Power (dehydration-induced uterine inertia) are particularly common. The nurse midwife who can correctly attribute prolonged labour to Power, Passage, or Passenger will avoid the catastrophic error of administering oxytocin in the presence of obstruction.
176.def:
Definition

Prolonged Labour: Active phase labour lasting more than 12 hours (primigravida) or 8 hours (multigravida), caused by abnormalities of the three Ps — Power (uterine contractions), Passage (pelvis and soft tissues), or Passenger (fetal size or position) — resulting in cervical dilation of less than 1 cm per hour.

177.intro:
Introduction

Prolonged labour is a significant contributor to maternal exhaustion, postpartum haemorrhage, operative delivery, and perinatal asphyxia, making it a priority topic in MUHS obstetric nursing. It is defined by failure of normal progress through the active phase of labour and must be distinguished from the latent phase, which can normally last up to 20 hours. The nurse midwife plays a frontline role in monitoring progress using the partograph and escalating care when the alert line is crossed.
177.def:
Definition

Prolonged Labour: Labour in which the active phase lasts more than 12 hours in a primigravida or 8 hours in a multigravida, with cervical dilation less than 1 cm per hour; identified by the partograph crossing the alert line.

178.intro:
Introduction

Malpresentation — any presentation other than vertex (cephalic with occiput as denominator) — complicates approximately 4–5% of deliveries at term and is a leading indication for caesarean section in India. Breech presentation is the most common type, occurring in 3–4% of term pregnancies, and carries significant risks including cord prolapse, head entrapment, and birth asphyxia. The nurse midwife must be competent in antenatal detection, understanding of management options including external cephalic version (ECV), and safe intrapartum care.
178.def:
Definition

Malpresentation: Any presentation of the fetus at the pelvic inlet other than the vertex (occipito-anterior); includes breech (buttocks/feet), face, brow, shoulder (transverse lie), and compound presentations. Breech presentation is the most common, with the fetal buttocks or lower limbs occupying the lower pole of the uterus.

179.intro:
Introduction

Breech presentation, occurring in 3–4% of term pregnancies, represents the single most common malpresentation encountered in clinical obstetric practice in India. It is associated with significant maternal and fetal risk, primarily due to the hazard of aftercoming head entrapment and cord prolapse. With ultrasonography now widely available at secondary care level, early detection and counselling regarding external cephalic version (ECV) or planned caesarean section are key responsibilities of the trained nurse midwife.
179.def:
Definition

Breech Presentation: A longitudinal lie in which the buttocks or lower limbs of the fetus occupy the lower uterine segment and present at the pelvic inlet; classified as frank breech (hips flexed, knees extended — 65%), complete breech (hips and knees flexed — 25%), and footling/incomplete breech (one or both feet presenting — 10%).

180.intro:
Introduction

Malpresentations collectively account for a disproportionately high share of operative deliveries and perinatal mortality in India, with breech being the most common and face, brow, and shoulder presentations presenting increasingly complex management challenges. The ability to detect malpresentation antenatally through abdominal examination and ultrasonography, and to understand the mode of delivery for each type, is a core MUHS nursing competency. Timely referral to a facility with surgical capacity is the single most important intervention when malpresentation is identified.
180.def:
Definition

Malpresentation: Any fetal presentation at the pelvic inlet other than vertex; common types include breech (buttocks or feet, most common), face (fully extended head, mentum as denominator), brow (partially extended, always requires caesarean section due to 13.5 cm mentovertical diameter), and transverse/shoulder lie (always caesarean section at term).

181.intro:
Introduction

Occipitoposterior (OP) position, in which the fetal occiput faces the maternal sacrum, is the most common malposition encountered in labour, complicating approximately 10–15% of vertex presentations. It is characterised by severe backache, prolonged first and second stages, and an early urge to push before full cervical dilation. While 65% rotate spontaneously to the occiput anterior position, persistent OP requires operative intervention and the nurse midwife must provide skilled support, positional management, and continuous monitoring throughout.
181.def:
Definition

Occipitoposterior (OP) Position: A malposition in which the fetal occiput (back of the head) faces the maternal sacrum (posterior), resulting in a “back-to-back” labour characterised by severe backache, slow cervical dilation, and prolonged labour; the most common malposition in vertex presentations.

182.intro:
Introduction

Contracted pelvis, in which one or more pelvic diameters are reduced below the minimum required for safe vaginal delivery, remains a significant cause of obstructed labour and maternal mortality in India, particularly among women with a history of rickets, malnutrition, or short stature. Early identification through clinical pelvimetry and ultrasound, especially at the first antenatal visit, enables appropriate birth planning. Management in the second stage requires careful trial of labour assessment or timely caesarean section to prevent catastrophic outcomes including uterine rupture and fistula formation.
182.def:
Definition

Contracted Pelvis: A pelvis in which one or more diameters are reduced below the obstetric minimum; the normal anteroposterior (AP) diameter of the inlet is 11 cm, and a diameter of <10 cm is contracted while <8 cm is absolutely contracted, requiring elective caesarean section regardless of other factors.

183.intro:
Introduction

Obstetric emergencies are sudden, life-threatening complications of pregnancy and labour that require immediate, coordinated action to prevent maternal and fetal death. In India, they remain a leading contributor to the maternal mortality ratio (MMR) of approximately 97 per 100,000 live births (SRS 2018–2020), with haemorrhage, eclampsia, and sepsis topping the list. The nurse midwife is often the first responder and must be trained to recognise, initiate first-line treatment, and mobilise the team rapidly for conditions such as cord prolapse, uterine rupture, and amniotic fluid embolism.
183.def:
Definition

Obstetric Emergency: Any acute, life-threatening condition occurring during pregnancy, labour, or the puerperium that requires immediate medical and nursing intervention to prevent maternal or fetal death; includes eclampsia, postpartum haemorrhage (PPH), cord prolapse, shoulder dystocia, amniotic fluid embolism (AFE), uterine rupture, and uterine inversion.

184.intro:
Introduction

Obstetric emergencies represent the most time-critical situations in midwifery practice, where a delay of even a few minutes can determine the difference between life and death for the mother and baby. India’s Pradhan Mantri Surakshit Matritva Abhiyan (PMSMA) and LaQshya programmes specifically mandate nurse midwife training in emergency obstetric care (EmOC) to address the preventable maternal mortality burden. The nurse midwife’s role extends from early recognition and first-line stabilisation to documentation, communication, and post-event debriefing.
184.def:
Definition

Obstetric Emergency: A sudden, life-threatening complication arising during pregnancy, labour, or the puerperium requiring immediate skilled intervention; categorised as antepartum (APH, eclampsia, cord prolapse), intrapartum (shoulder dystocia, uterine rupture, AFE), and postpartum (PPH, uterine inversion, puerperal sepsis).

185.intro:
Introduction

The ability to enumerate and manage obstetric emergencies is a core clinical competency tested repeatedly in MUHS examinations, reflecting its critical importance in reducing India’s preventable maternal mortality. Cord prolapse, one of the most acute of all obstetric emergencies, demands a fetal head displaced off the cord within minutes to prevent irreversible hypoxic brain injury or death. The nurse midwife’s general responsibilities across all emergencies follow a universal framework: recognise, call for help, initiate first-line treatment, monitor, document, and refer.
185.def:
Definition

Cord Prolapse: Descent of the umbilical cord past the presenting part after rupture of membranes, resulting in cord compression between the presenting part and the maternal pelvis; a true obstetric emergency with a fetal mortality of up to 91% if not delivered within minutes. Cord presentation (cord felt through intact membranes) is the precursor state.

186.intro:
Introduction

Cord prolapse is among the most time-sensitive obstetric emergencies, with fetal prognosis directly dependent on the interval between diagnosis and delivery. In India, it most commonly occurs in association with malpresentation, polyhydramnios, and injudicious artificial rupture of membranes (ARM) with an unengaged head — situations frequently encountered in secondary care settings. The nurse midwife must be able to distinguish cord presentation (intact membranes) from cord prolapse (post-ROM), initiate the PUSH emergency sequence immediately, and facilitate emergency caesarean section or instrumental delivery without delay.

187.intro:
Introduction

Management of cord prolapse requires simultaneous execution of multiple emergency interventions, making it one of the most challenging yet teachable scenarios in obstetric nursing. The principle is simple: relieve cord compression by elevating the presenting part and expedite delivery. In Indian district hospitals and PHCs, the nurse midwife is often the only skilled birth attendant present at the moment of diagnosis and must act within seconds without waiting for a physician. Competency in the PUSH sequence is therefore non-negotiable for all trained nurse midwives in India.
187.def:
Definition

Cord Prolapse: Descent of the umbilical cord below or alongside the presenting part after rupture of membranes, leading to cord compression and acute fetal hypoxia; occult prolapse (cord lies alongside the presenting part, not visible) is detected by variable decelerations on CTG and confirmed on vaginal examination.

188.intro:
Introduction

Amniotic fluid embolism (AFE) is one of the rarest yet most catastrophic obstetric emergencies, with a reported mortality of 60–80% and no reliable method of prevention or prediction. It occurs when amniotic fluid, fetal cells, hair, or other debris enters the maternal pulmonary circulation, triggering an anaphylactoid response and catastrophic cardiorespiratory collapse followed by disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC). The nurse midwife must recognise the classic triad immediately and initiate CPR & ICU transfer without delay, as survival is entirely dependent on speed of resuscitation.
188.def:
Definition

Amniotic Fluid Embolism (AFE): A rare but catastrophic obstetric emergency in which amniotic fluid, fetal squamous cells, lanugo, or vernix enter the maternal bloodstream through uterine or cervical veins, triggering an anaphylactoid reaction characterised by acute respiratory failure, cardiovascular collapse, and disseminated intravascular coagulation (DIC); mortality 60–80%.

189.intro:
Introduction

Acute uterine inversion is a rare but life-threatening obstetric emergency occurring in the third stage of labour, predominantly due to mismanagement of the cord control (cord traction before placental separation or fundal pressure on an atonic uterus). It results in severe neurogenic and haemorrhagic shock and must be corrected within minutes to prevent maternal death. In India, where mismanaged third stage in home deliveries and under-resourced facilities remains a concern, the nurse midwife’s knowledge of prevention and immediate management is critical.
189.def:
Definition

Uterine Inversion: A rare obstetric emergency in which the uterine fundus collapses inward and turns inside out, protruding through the cervix into or beyond the vagina during the third stage of labour; classified as incomplete (fundus not beyond the cervix), complete (fundus beyond the cervix), or prolapsed (fundus visible at the vulva); most commonly caused by cord traction before placental separation.

190.intro:
Introduction

Fetal distress, now more precisely termed non-reassuring fetal status, reflects acute or chronic fetal hypoxia during labour and is one of the most common indications for emergency operative delivery in India. It is detected through continuous monitoring of the fetal heart rate (FHR) using a CTG or Pinard’s stethoscope, and by assessment of amniotic fluid colour. The nurse midwife must be able to recognise the hallmarks of fetal distress—bradycardia, late decelerations, and thick meconium—and immediately initiate intrauterine resuscitation while summoning obstetric support.
190.def:
Definition

Fetal Distress (Non-Reassuring Fetal Status): A clinical condition in which the fetus shows signs of inadequate oxygenation during pregnancy or labour, manifested as fetal heart rate <110 or >160 bpm, late decelerations, absent beat-to-beat variability on CTG, and/or meconium-stained amniotic fluid; requires immediate assessment and intervention to prevent fetal hypoxic injury or death.

191.intro:
Introduction

Injuries to the birth canal are common intrapartum complications that, if undetected or inadequately repaired, contribute to postpartum haemorrhage (trauma is one of the 4 T’s of PPH), long-term pelvic floor dysfunction, and anal incontinence. In India, where instrumental deliveries and deliveries without skilled attendance are more prevalent, birth canal injuries including cervical tears, vaginal lacerations, and third- and fourth-degree perineal tears remain a significant problem. The nurse midwife must systematically inspect the entire birth canal after every delivery and promptly repair or refer injuries as appropriate.
191.def:
Definition

Birth Canal Injuries: Traumatic lacerations or tears occurring to the cervix, vagina, perineum, or vulva during the second stage of labour; classified by location (cervical, vaginal, perineal) and severity; perineal tears are graded 1st–4th degree based on depth of tissue involvement, with 3rd degree (anal sphincter) and 4th degree (rectal mucosa) requiring specialist operative repair.

192.intro:
Introduction

Perineal tears are among the most frequently encountered complications of vaginal delivery, occurring in up to 85% of first-time mothers. While first- and second-degree tears are manageable at the bedside, third- and fourth-degree obstetric anal sphincter injuries (OASIS) carry a risk of long-term anal incontinence and significantly impact quality of life. Prevention through controlled delivery of the fetal head and selective episiotomy, combined with meticulous repair and postoperative care, are key nursing responsibilities mandated under MUHS midwifery curriculum.
192.def:
Definition

Perineal Tear: A spontaneous laceration of the perineal body occurring during the second stage of labour, classified into four degrees: 1st degree (skin and vaginal mucosa only), 2nd degree (skin and perineal muscles, anal sphincter intact), 3rd degree (anal sphincter involved — 3a: <50%, 3b: >50%, 3c: internal anal sphincter), and 4th degree (sphincter plus rectal mucosa).

193.intro:
Introduction

Episiotomy—a deliberate surgical incision of the perineum to enlarge the vaginal outlet at delivery—was once performed routinely in India but is now recommended only on a selective basis by WHO (2018) and the Indian Council of Medical Research (ICMR), as routine use has been shown to increase the risk of third- and fourth-degree tears. The nurse midwife must understand the current evidence-based indications, the correct technique for mediolateral incision, meticulous three-layer repair, and comprehensive postoperative perineal care to promote healing and prevent infection.
193.def:
Definition

Episiotomy: A deliberate, planned surgical incision of the perineum and posterior vaginal wall made during the second stage of labour at crowning to enlarge the vaginal outlet and facilitate delivery; performed under local anaesthesia (1% lignocaine) and repaired in three layers using absorbable sutures; types include mediolateral (45° from midline, most common in India) and median/midline.

194.intro:
Introduction

Induction of labour (IOL) is one of the most commonly performed obstetric interventions in India, accounting for approximately 20–25% of all deliveries in institutional settings, with rates rising due to increased detection of post-datism, gestational diabetes, and hypertensive disorders through enhanced antenatal surveillance. The nurse midwife’s role in IOL encompasses pre-induction assessment using the Bishop Score, safe administration of oxytocin under continuous CTG monitoring, early recognition of complications such as uterine hyperstimulation, and preparedness for emergency caesarean section in case of failed induction.
194.def:
Definition

Induction of Labour (IOL): The artificial stimulation of uterine contractions before the spontaneous onset of labour to achieve vaginal delivery; indicated when continuation of pregnancy poses greater risk to mother or fetus than delivery; requires a Bishop Score ≥6 (favourable cervix) for successful induction, or prior cervical ripening if Bishop Score <6.

195.intro:
Introduction

Ventouse (vacuum-assisted) delivery is an instrumental method used to expedite delivery during the second stage of labour when maternal pushing is insufficient or when rapid delivery is required for fetal or maternal indications. It is preferred over forceps in many Indian tertiary centres due to significantly lower rates of maternal perineal trauma, though it carries a unique risk of neonatal scalp injuries including cephalhaematoma and the potentially fatal subgaleal haemorrhage. The nurse midwife assists with preparation, monitoring, and neonatal assessment during and after ventouse delivery.
195.def:
Definition

Ventouse (Vacuum-Assisted) Delivery: An instrumental obstetric procedure in which a suction cup is applied to the fetal scalp at the flexion point (3 cm anterior to the posterior fontanelle) and negative pressure (0.6–0.8 kg/cm²) is created to allow traction and rotation during maternal expulsive efforts; requires full cervical dilation, engaged vertex presentation, and ruptured membranes.

196.intro:
Introduction

Forceps delivery is an instrumental obstetric procedure with a long history in midwifery, enabling assisted vaginal delivery when maternal effort alone is insufficient in the second stage or when urgent delivery is required to prevent maternal or fetal compromise. In India, forceps use has declined with the rise of vacuum extraction and increased caesarean rates, but remains an essential skill for complex situations such as aftercoming head in breech delivery and rotation of the persistent occipitoposterior position. The nurse midwife plays a critical preparatory and monitoring role during all forceps deliveries.
196.def:
Definition

Forceps Delivery: An instrumental obstetric procedure in which specially designed metal traction instruments are applied to the fetal head to assist delivery during the second stage of labour; types include outlet forceps (Wrigley’s — head visible), low forceps (Simpson’s — station +2 or below), and mid-cavity rotational forceps (Kielland’s — for OP rotation); requires full dilation, engaged cephalic presentation, and adequate analgesia.

197.intro:
Introduction

Caesarean section (C-section) is the most commonly performed major surgery in obstetrics worldwide and in India, where the national C-section rate has risen to approximately 21.5% (NFHS-5, 2019–21), well above the WHO-recommended 10–15%. While it is life-saving when indicated, unnecessary C-sections carry significant maternal risks including haemorrhage, infection, anaesthetic complications, and risks to future pregnancies. The nurse midwife must provide high-quality pre-operative preparation and systematic post-operative care to minimise complications and support early recovery and breastfeeding.
197.def:
Definition

Caesarean Section: A surgical procedure in which the fetus is delivered through incisions in the abdominal wall (laparotomy) and uterus (hysterotomy); the most common type is the Lower Segment Caesarean Section (LSCS), using a transverse incision on the lower uterine segment, which causes less blood loss, heals more securely, and allows subsequent vaginal birth after caesarean (VBAC).

198.intro:
Introduction

Caesarean section is the most frequently performed major operative procedure in Indian obstetric units, with rates rising steeply in both public and private sectors. Understanding its types, indications, and perioperative nursing management is essential for the MUHS-trained nurse midwife who will be involved in pre-operative preparation, intraoperative scrub or circulating duties, and the full spectrum of post-operative recovery care. Recognition and early management of post-caesarean complications—including wound infection, paralytic ileus, and deep vein thrombosis—are equally important nursing competencies.
198.def:
Definition

Caesarean Section: Delivery of the fetus via surgical incision through the abdominal wall and uterus; performed as elective (planned, non-emergency), emergency (immediate threat to mother or fetus), or urgent (compromise not immediately life-threatening); the LSCS (lower segment transverse incision) accounts for 95% of all caesarean sections and is the preferred type in contemporary practice.

199.intro:
Introduction

India’s caesarean section rate has more than doubled over the past two decades, rising from 8.5% in NFHS-3 (2005–06) to 21.5% in NFHS-5 (2019–21), with private hospitals recording rates as high as 47.4%. This far exceeds the WHO-recommended ceiling of 10–15%, raising concerns about unnecessary surgical risk, increased healthcare costs, and implications for future pregnancies including placenta accreta spectrum. The nurse midwife plays an important advocacy and preparatory role, supporting VBAC where appropriate, counselling women against unnecessary C-sections, and ensuring meticulous perioperative care when surgery is genuinely indicated.
199.def:
Definition

Caesarean Section Rate: The proportion of all live births delivered by caesarean section in a given population; the WHO (2015) recommends a population-level C-section rate of 10–15%, below which maternal and perinatal mortality decreases with increasing caesarean availability, and above which no further mortality benefit is demonstrated but risks of surgical morbidity increase.

200.intro:
Introduction

The indications for caesarean section encompass a broad spectrum of maternal, fetal, and placental conditions, and their correct identification is one of the most important clinical decision-making competencies in obstetric nursing. In India, understanding both absolute indications (where vaginal delivery is impossible or unsafe) and relative indications (where clinical judgement weighs risks and benefits) is essential for the nurse midwife who must assist obstetricians in decision-making, prepare families for emergency procedures, and contribute to audit and quality improvement in reducing unnecessary caesarean rates.
200.def:
Definition

Indications for Caesarean Section: Clinical conditions in which caesarean delivery is necessary or preferable to vaginal delivery to protect maternal or fetal health; classified as absolute (vaginal delivery impossible or life-threatening — e.g., major placenta praevia, cord prolapse, severe CPD, transverse lie at term) or relative (C-section preferable given the clinical context — e.g., fetal distress, breech, failed induction, previous classical scar).

201.intro:
Introduction

Per vaginal (PV) examination is a fundamental obstetric assessment skill performed during labour to evaluate progress and foetal descent. In Indian maternity units, it guides decisions about augmentation, referral, and mode of delivery. The nurse midwife must perform PV examination aseptically, systematically, and with informed consent to ensure safe intrapartum monitoring.
201.def:
Definition

Per Vaginal (PV) Examination: A digital examination of the birth canal performed to assess cervical dilation and effacement, station and presentation of the presenting part, status of membranes, moulding, and adequacy of the bony pelvis — typically repeated every 4 hours during active labour.

202.intro:
Introduction

The Bishop Score is the standard pre-induction cervical assessment tool used in obstetric units across India to predict the likelihood of successful labour induction. An unfavourable cervix (score <6) leads to high induction failure rates and unnecessary maternal risk. Nurse midwives must calculate this score accurately and communicate findings before any induction attempt.
202.def:
Definition

Bishop Score: A pre-labour cervical scoring system assessing five parameters — dilation, effacement, station, consistency, and position of the cervix — with a maximum score of 13; a score ≥6 indicates a favourable cervix suitable for induction of labour.

203.intro:
Introduction

Injection Magnesium Sulphate (MgSO4) is the drug of choice for both prevention and treatment of eclamptic convulsions — a leading cause of maternal death in India. Its safe administration requires precise knowledge of the Pritchard regimen and vigilant monitoring of toxic effects. Every nurse midwife in an obstetric setting must be competent in MgSO4 therapy and must be ready to administer the antidote, calcium gluconate, at the bedside.
203.def:
Definition

Magnesium Sulphate (MgSO4): An inorganic salt that acts as a CNS depressant and smooth muscle relaxant; used as the first-line anticonvulsant in severe pre-eclampsia and eclampsia, administered via the Pritchard regimen (4 g IV loading + 5 g IM each buttock, followed by 5 g IM maintenance every 4 hours).

204.intro:
Introduction

Injection Methylergometrine (Methergin) is an ergot alkaloid oxytocic routinely used in the management of postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) and as part of active management of the third stage of labour in Indian hospitals. Its potent vasoconstrictive action makes it highly effective for uterine atony but potentially dangerous in hypertensive patients. The nurse must always check blood pressure before administration and store the drug at 2–8°C.
204.def:
Definition

Methylergometrine (Methergin): A semi-synthetic ergot alkaloid that produces sustained tetanic contraction of the uterus by direct action on uterine smooth muscle; given as 0.2 mg IM or slow IV for treatment of atonic PPH — contraindicated in hypertension, pre-eclampsia, and heart disease.

205.intro:
Introduction

Tocolytic agents are drugs that suppress uterine contractions to delay preterm birth by at least 48 hours, allowing time for antenatal corticosteroid administration to mature foetal lungs. Preterm birth is a major contributor to neonatal mortality in India, and the nurse midwife plays a critical role in monitoring the mother and foetus for adverse effects during tocolytic therapy.
205.def:
Definition

Tocolytic Agents: Pharmacological agents used to inhibit uterine contractions in preterm labour (before 37 weeks), with the goal of delaying delivery by 48 hours to enable betamethasone (antenatal corticosteroid) administration for foetal lung maturity; first-line agent in India is nifedipine (calcium channel blocker).

206.intro:
Introduction

Puerperal sepsis remains a major preventable cause of maternal mortality in India, accounting for approximately 11% of maternal deaths. It arises from genital tract infection following delivery or abortion, often due to poor asepsis, prolonged labour, or retained products. Early recognition and aggressive treatment with broad-spectrum antibiotics are essential nursing and medical responsibilities during the postpartum period.

207.intro:
Introduction

The puerperium — the six weeks following delivery — is a period of significant physiological change and potential complications. In Indian obstetric practice, complications such as PPH, puerperal sepsis, DVT, breast problems, and postpartum psychiatric disorders are commonly encountered. The nurse midwife must perform systematic daily assessment to detect and manage these complications early, thereby reducing maternal morbidity.
207.def:
Definition

Puerperal Morbidity: Temperature of 38°C (100.4°F) or more occurring on any two of the first ten days postpartum, exclusive of the first 24 hours, taken by mouth by a standard technique at least four times daily (ACOG/WHO definition); indicates the presence of a complication requiring investigation.

208.intro:
Introduction

Puerperal pyrexia (postpartum fever) is a common yet potentially serious finding in the puerperium, with causes ranging from uterine infection and UTI to DVT and mastitis. In Indian healthcare settings where infection-related maternal deaths remain high, the nurse midwife must systematically identify the source of fever, collect appropriate specimens, and initiate management without delay to prevent progression to septicaemia.
208.def:
Definition

Puerperal Pyrexia: A fever of ≥38°C (100.4°F) occurring on any two of the first ten days postpartum (excluding the first 24 hours), investigated to identify the underlying source — most commonly uterine infection, urinary tract infection, wound infection, mastitis, or deep vein thrombosis.

209.intro:
Introduction

Prevention of puerperal sepsis is a priority for obstetric nurses and midwives, particularly in India where infection remains a leading cause of maternal death. The majority of puerperal infections are preventable through strict aseptic technique, hand hygiene, limiting vaginal examinations, and prophylactic antibiotics. Implementing these measures at every delivery reduces maternal morbidity and mortality significantly.
209.def:
Definition

Puerperal Sepsis: Infection of the genital tract occurring between the onset of rupture of membranes or labour and the 42nd day postpartum, in which two or more of the following are present — pelvic pain, fever (≥38.5°C), abnormal vaginal discharge, delayed uterine involution (WHO definition); prevention focuses on breaking the chain of infection at every point of care.
209.conclusion:
Conclusion

Prevention of puerperal sepsis is a core nursing competency under MUHS and INC guidelines. The nurse midwife must champion hand hygiene, enforce aseptic technique at every delivery, minimise unnecessary vaginal examinations, ensure timely placental delivery, and administer prophylactic antibiotics for operative deliveries. These measures form the first line of defence against a condition that remains a leading cause of preventable maternal death in India.

210.intro:
Introduction

The normal puerperium spans the first six weeks after delivery, during which the body undergoes involution to return to its pre-pregnant state. While this is a physiological process, minor complications such as afterpains, breast engorgement, perineal discomfort, and constipation are common and require nursing intervention. In Indian maternity practice, the nurse midwife's systematic postnatal assessment and health education are key to ensuring a smooth recovery.

211.intro:
Introduction

Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is the single largest cause of maternal mortality worldwide and in India, accounting for approximately 27% of maternal deaths. It requires immediate recognition and systematic stepwise management. The midwife plays a frontline role in preventing PPH through active management of the third stage of labour (AMTSL) and in managing it through fundal massage, oxytocin administration, and timely referral.

212.intro:
Introduction

Postpartum haemorrhage (PPH) is classified by its timing into primary (<24 hours) and secondary (24 hours to 6 weeks) types, each with distinct causes and management approaches. Uterine atony, the most common cause, accounts for 70% of primary PPH cases in Indian obstetric units. Understanding PPH types and their stepwise management is among the highest-yield topics in MUHS midwifery examinations.
212.def:
Definition

Postpartum Haemorrhage (PPH): Blood loss of ≥500 mL following vaginal delivery or ≥1000 mL following caesarean section, occurring within 24 hours (primary PPH) or between 24 hours and 6 weeks after delivery (secondary PPH); the most common cause of primary PPH is uterine atony (tone — 70%).

213.intro:
Introduction

Postpartum haemorrhage is largely preventable through active management of the third stage of labour (AMTSL), which has reduced PPH incidence by up to 60% in Indian trials. Identifying women at high risk — grand multipara, multiple pregnancy, prolonged labour — and ensuring AMTSL at every delivery are core nursing responsibilities. When PPH does occur, rapid stepwise management saves lives.
213.def:
Definition

PPH — True (Atonic) PPH: Blood loss ≥500 mL after vaginal delivery, where the uterus fails to contract and retract following delivery of the placenta (uterine atony), leading to persistent haemorrhage from the placental bed; the uterus feels soft and boggy on palpation and does not contract with fundal massage.

214.intro:
Introduction

Recognising the early signs and symptoms of PPH is a critical nursing competency, as haemodynamic decompensation can be rapid and life-threatening. In Indian maternity units with limited blood bank access, the nurse midwife must act within minutes — calling for help, rubbing up the uterus, and establishing IV access — before the patient enters irreversible shock. Timely recognition and escalation determine survival.
214.def:
Definition

Primary PPH: Loss of 500 mL or more of blood from the genital tract within 24 hours of delivery; signs include a boggy uterus, excessive vaginal bleeding, rising pulse, falling blood pressure, pallor, and cold clammy skin, indicating haemodynamic compromise that requires immediate intervention.

215.intro:
Introduction

Case-based questions in MUHS examinations test the nurse's ability to apply theoretical knowledge to a clinical scenario. In this case, Mrs. S delivered 2 hours ago and is reporting excessive bleeding with repeated pad changes — a classic presentation of primary PPH. The nurse must immediately assess uterine tone, estimate blood loss, initiate management, and escalate care, demonstrating competency in obstetric emergency response.
215.def:
Definition

Postpartum Haemorrhage (PPH): Blood loss ≥500 mL after vaginal delivery occurring within 24 hours (primary) or 24 hours to 6 weeks (secondary); in Mrs. S's case, changing pads three times in 2 hours suggests significant blood loss consistent with primary PPH, most likely due to uterine atony.

216.intro:
Introduction

Complications of PPH extend well beyond the immediate postpartum period. Hypovolaemic shock, Sheehan's syndrome (pituitary necrosis due to haemorrhage), acute renal failure, and DIC can have long-lasting consequences for the mother's health. In Indian healthcare settings where blood transfusion services may be delayed, the nurse midwife must anticipate these complications and initiate timely resuscitation to prevent irreversible organ damage.
216.def:
Definition

Puerperium: The period from delivery of the placenta to 6 weeks (42 days) postpartum during which the reproductive organs return to their pre-pregnant state; complications occurring during this period — including PPH, sepsis, thromboembolism, and psychiatric disorders — are collectively termed puerperal morbidity.

217.intro:
Introduction

Breast problems in the puerperal period are a common reason for early cessation of breastfeeding in India, contributing to suboptimal infant nutrition. Conditions such as engorgement, cracked nipples, mastitis, and breast abscess form a progression that can be largely prevented by correct breastfeeding technique and demand feeding. The nurse midwife's role in early lactation support and education is pivotal for both maternal and neonatal wellbeing.
217.def:
Definition

Puerperal Breast Complications: A spectrum of breast disorders arising during the postnatal period, ranging from engorgement (milk stasis with bilateral breast swelling and pain, typically day 3–4), to mastitis (acute bacterial infection with fever, localised tenderness, and erythema), to breast abscess (fluctuant collection of pus requiring incision and drainage).

218.intro:
Introduction

Breast engorgement is one of the most common problems encountered in the early postnatal period, typically occurring on day 3–4 when colostrum transitions to mature milk. If not managed promptly, it can lead to painful feeding, poor latching, and eventual abandonment of breastfeeding. In Indian public health settings, the nurse midwife must educate mothers about demand feeding and correct latching from the first hour after birth to prevent this complication.
218.def:
Definition

Breast Engorgement: Painful, bilateral overfilling of the breasts with milk and oedema fluid, occurring 3–4 days postpartum due to milk stasis; characterised by hard, tense, warm, shiny breasts with flattened nipples that impede latching — managed primarily by frequent, effective breastfeeding on demand.

219.intro:
Introduction

A structured, tiered approach to neonatal care ensures that every newborn receives care appropriate to its clinical needs and that resources are used efficiently. India's national health programme recognises three levels of neonatal care — from basic essential newborn care at the primary level to full NICU services at tertiary centres. The nurse midwife must understand these levels to make appropriate referral decisions and provide level-specific care.
219.def:
Definition

Levels of Neonatal Care (NHM India): A three-tiered framework — Level I (basic/essential care at PHC for stable term babies), Level II (Special Newborn Care Unit/SNCU at district hospitals for babies ≥32 weeks or ≥1500 g needing IV fluids, phototherapy, or CPAP), and Level III (NICU at tertiary hospitals for babies <32 weeks or <1500 g requiring ventilation and surgery).

220.intro:
Introduction

Prematurity is the leading cause of neonatal mortality in India, contributing to over 35% of neonatal deaths. A premature baby born before 37 weeks of gestation faces multiple physiological challenges including respiratory distress, hypothermia, hypoglycaemia, and sepsis due to organ immaturity. The nurse midwife's systematic care — particularly thermoregulation through kangaroo mother care and promotion of breast milk feeding — is the cornerstone of improving preterm survival.
220.def:
Definition

Premature Baby (Preterm Neonate): A newborn delivered before 37 completed weeks of gestation (259 days from the first day of the last menstrual period), regardless of birth weight; further classified as extremely preterm (<28 wks), very preterm (28–32 wks), and moderate/late preterm (32–37 wks), with morbidity inversely proportional to gestational age.

221.intro:
Introduction

Nursing care of the preterm baby is a specialised, high-stakes responsibility requiring vigilance across multiple organ systems simultaneously. Preterm neonates are physiologically vulnerable to hypothermia, hypoglycaemia, sepsis, and respiratory failure, and their outcomes depend heavily on the quality of nursing care provided at the bedside. In Indian SNCU and NICU settings, the nurse is often the first to detect early signs of deterioration and must respond immediately.
221.def:
Definition

Preterm Neonate: A baby born at fewer than 37 completed weeks of gestation; nursing care of the preterm baby encompasses thermoregulation (target axillary temperature 36.5–37.5°C), nutritional support (exclusive breast milk via orogastric tube if unable to suck), infection prevention, respiratory support, and developmental care to minimise stress and support neurodevelopment.

222.intro:
Introduction

Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC) is a WHO-recommended, evidence-based intervention that has transformed the survival of low birth weight and preterm babies, particularly in resource-limited settings like India. By providing continuous skin-to-skin contact between mother and baby, KMC maintains thermal stability, promotes exclusive breastfeeding, and strengthens mother–infant bonding. Its implementation in district hospitals and SNCUs under the national health programme makes it a key MUHS examination topic.
222.def:
Definition

Kangaroo Mother Care (KMC): A method of caring for preterm and low birth weight neonates (<2000 g) by providing continuous skin-to-skin contact between the baby (placed prone, upright, between the mother's breasts in a frog position) and the mother or caregiver, combined with exclusive breastfeeding — shown to reduce neonatal mortality by up to 40% (WHO, 2022).

223.intro:
Introduction

Post-term or post-dates pregnancy (beyond 42 weeks) carries increasing fetal risk due to placental insufficiency, oligohydramnios, and meconium passage. In India, where accurate dating by first-trimester ultrasound is not always available, recognition of postmaturity signs in the newborn is an essential clinical skill. The nurse midwife must recognise the stigmata of postmaturity in the neonate and anticipate complications such as meconium aspiration syndrome.
223.def:
Definition

Postmaturity: A pregnancy that has exceeded 42 completed weeks (294 days) from the first day of the last menstrual period (WHO definition); the postmature neonate shows characteristic features of placental dysfunction including dry peeling skin, absence of vernix and lanugo, meconium staining, and a wasted, alert appearance.

224.intro:
Introduction

Postmaturity exerts dual adverse effects on both the fetus and the mother by prolonging exposure to a progressively failing placenta. As gestational age exceeds 42 weeks, amniotic fluid decreases, fetal reserve diminishes, and the risk of intrauterine death rises sharply. In Indian obstetric practice, the nurse midwife must counsel families about the risks and assist in implementing surveillance and timely induction to prevent adverse perinatal outcomes.
224.def:
Definition

Postmaturity Syndrome (Clifford Syndrome): A clinical condition in the neonate born after 42 weeks, characterised by decreased subcutaneous fat, dry cracked peeling skin, meconium staining of skin/nails/cord, and an alert hypervigilant expression — resulting from chronic uteroplacental insufficiency and intrauterine malnutrition.

225.intro:
Introduction

Asphyxia neonatorum is a neonatal emergency defined by failure to establish spontaneous respiration at birth, leading to hypoxia, hypercarbia, and acidosis. It contributes to approximately 23% of neonatal deaths in India. Skilled birth attendance and immediate neonatal resuscitation following the Neonatal Resuscitation Programme (NRP) algorithm are the most effective interventions, and every nurse midwife must be competent in basic neonatal resuscitation.
225.def:
Definition

Asphyxia Neonatorum: Failure of the newborn to initiate or sustain spontaneous respiration at birth, resulting in hypoxia and metabolic acidosis; clinically assessed by an Apgar score <7 at 1 minute — classified as mild (Apgar 6–7), moderate (4–5), or severe (≤3) — requiring graded resuscitation per the NRP algorithm.

226.intro:
Introduction

Neonatal jaundice requiring phototherapy is one of the most common indications for admission to special care nurseries in India. Phototherapy uses blue-spectrum light (420–470 nm) to photo-isomerise unconjugated bilirubin into a water-soluble form that can be excreted without hepatic conjugation. The nurse caring for a baby under phototherapy must balance effective treatment with prevention of complications such as dehydration, hypothermia, and retinal damage.
226.def:
Definition

Phototherapy: The therapeutic use of blue-spectrum light (wavelength 420–470 nm) to convert unconjugated (indirect) bilirubin in the skin into water-soluble photoisomers (lumirubin) that are excreted in bile and urine without requiring hepatic conjugation; indicated when serum bilirubin reaches treatment threshold levels based on gestational age and postnatal age (AAP nomogram).

227.intro:
Introduction

Haemolytic disease of the newborn (HDN) results from maternal antibodies crossing the placenta and destroying fetal red blood cells, leading to haemolytic jaundice. Rh incompatibility is the most severe form and was historically a major cause of neonatal death and disability in India until the introduction of Anti-D immunoglobulin prophylaxis. Prompt recognition and management of haemolytic jaundice prevents the devastating neurological complication of kernicterus.
227.def:
Definition

Haemolytic Disease of the Newborn (HDN): A condition caused by incompatibility between maternal and fetal blood groups (most commonly Rh or ABO), in which maternal IgG antibodies cross the placenta and cause immune-mediated destruction of fetal/neonatal red blood cells, resulting in anaemia, unconjugated hyperbilirubinaemia (haemolytic jaundice), and — if untreated — kernicterus.

228.intro:
Introduction

Prevention of HIV transmission from mother to child (PMTCT) is a national priority in India under the Prevention of Parent-to-Child Transmission (PPTCT) programme. Without intervention, vertical transmission rates range from 15–45%; with optimal PPTCT, this can be reduced to below 2%. The nurse midwife is central to counselling, HIV testing at the first ANC visit, ensuring ART compliance, administering neonatal prophylaxis, and guiding safe infant feeding decisions.
228.def:
Definition

PMTCT (Prevention of Mother-to-Child Transmission): A comprehensive strategy to prevent vertical transmission of HIV from an infected mother to her baby during pregnancy, labour, delivery, or breastfeeding; implemented in India as the PPTCT programme, involving universal opt-out HIV testing in ANC, lifelong ART for the mother, and nevirapine prophylaxis for the neonate from birth.

229.intro:
Introduction

The question of whether HIV-positive mothers should breastfeed remains one of the most complex counselling challenges in obstetric nursing. In high-income countries, avoidance of breastfeeding is recommended; however, in Indian and other low-income settings, formula feeding carries its own risks including diarrhoeal disease and malnutrition. WHO guidelines, adopted by India's PPTCT programme, therefore recommend exclusive breastfeeding for six months with the mother on ART, rather than mixed or formula feeding.
229.def:
Definition

Breastfeeding in HIV: The decision framework for infant feeding by an HIV-positive mother, guided by the AFASS criteria (Affordable, Feasible, Acceptable, Sustainable, Safe); WHO recommends exclusive breastfeeding for 6 months with maternal ART when replacement feeding is not AFASS, as mixed feeding (breast + formula) paradoxically increases HIV transmission risk due to gut mucosal inflammation.

230.intro:
Introduction

Counselling an HIV-positive pregnant mother is a comprehensive nursing responsibility that encompasses clinical information-sharing, emotional support, and facilitation of informed decision-making. In India, stigma associated with HIV remains a major barrier to disclosure and treatment adherence.
